packs.title: The First Dinner
packs.blurb: For tables where people are still learning each other's names. Ten questions that are easy to walk into and hard to walk out of — no wrong answers, no homework, and everyone leaves knowing something real about everyone else.

cards.question: What's something you're good at that you never chose to be good at?
cards.followUp: Ask them: if you had gotten to choose, would you have picked something else?

cards.question: What's a rule you follow that you can't fully explain?
cards.followUp: Go around the table: who has the strangest one — and who's secretly glad their rule exists?

cards.question: Would you rather be confidently wrong or anxiously right?
cards.followUp: Ask which one they'd hire. Then ask which one they'd marry. The answers usually differ — ask why.

cards.question: What's the best compliment someone could give you that has nothing to do with your work?
cards.followUp: When answers stall, flip it: what compliment do you get all the time that means nothing to you?

cards.question: Is there a food, a song, or a place you love mostly because of who you were with the first time?
cards.followUp: Then ask: if the memory faded, would the love survive on its own?

cards.question: What do you believe that most people at this table probably don't?
cards.followUp: House rule: for two full minutes, nobody argues back — questions only. Watch how much further it goes.

cards.question: If your friends described you in one word behind your back, what word do you hope for — and what word do you suspect?
cards.followUp: The gap between those two answers is the real conversation. Ask about the gap.

cards.question: What's one small thing that instantly tells you a lot about a person?
cards.followUp: Test it on the spot: would anyone's signal wrongly condemn somebody at this table?

cards.question: Luck or effort — which one explains more of where you are tonight?
cards.followUp: Ask the person with the most impressive job to answer first. Then ask the person with the best life.

cards.question: What question do you wish people asked you more often?
cards.followUp: Then ask it — right now, out loud, to the person who said it.

packs.title: Home for the Holidays
packs.blurb: For the table you didn't choose and wouldn't trade. Questions built for three generations at once — grandparents can answer every one, teenagers will actually want to — and follow-ups for when somebody's uncle gets going.

cards.question: What's one rule from this family you've kept on purpose — and one you quietly dropped?
cards.followUp: Ask the oldest person here where the kept rule originally came from. It's usually older than anyone guesses.

cards.question: What's something your parents were right about that you hated hearing at the time?
cards.followUp: Now reverse it: what might the youngest person at this table be right about right now?

cards.question: Who from this family's past would you want at dinner tonight, and what would you ask them?
cards.followUp: Ask what they'd criticize about how we live now. Let it sting a little before anyone defends us.

cards.question: What's a family story that everyone tells slightly differently?
cards.followUp: Have two people tell it back to back, then ask the table: which of the differences actually matter?

cards.question: What did 'a good life' mean in this family fifty years ago — and does it still?
cards.followUp: Ask who at the table has come closest to it. First by the old definition, then by the new one.

cards.question: What's something you do exactly like your mother or father, even though you swore you never would?
cards.followUp: Ask: is that a habit you're stuck with, or one you'd defend? They're not the same thing.

cards.question: When is keeping the peace at this table the right call — and when is it just the easier one?
cards.followUp: No naming names: ask everyone for one topic they think this family avoids a little too well.

cards.question: Which tradition here would you fight to keep, even after it stopped being convenient?
cards.followUp: Ask: if we kept the tradition but forgot why we do it, would it still count?

cards.question: What do you owe family that you don't owe anyone else — and what do you not owe them, even if they think you do?
cards.followUp: Ask the person who moved farthest away to answer first.

cards.question: What's the kindest lie ever told at this table — and should it have been told?
cards.followUp: Keep it general if you need to. Then ask: who here claims they'd rather have the hard truth — and who's just saying that?

packs.title: Old Friends, Long Table
packs.blurb: For the people who knew you before you were this version of you. These questions spend the one currency new friends don't have — decades — and the follow-ups make sure nobody hides behind the old jokes.

cards.question: What did all of us believe back then that turned out to be completely wrong?
cards.followUp: Then ask: what do we all believe now that will look exactly the same in twenty years?

cards.question: Which past version of you would be most surprised by tonight — and pleasantly or not?
cards.followUp: Make everyone pick a specific age for their answer. The ages people choose are half the conversation.

cards.question: What's the biggest risk anyone at this table ever took — and honestly, did it pay off?
cards.followUp: Ask the risk-taker: knowing everything you know now, do you take it again? Then ask the table whether they should.

cards.question: Is anyone here actually the same person they were at twenty — and how would we know?
cards.followUp: Run the test: what would you have to lose — memories, habits, people — before this table said you weren't you anymore?

cards.question: What's something you envied in someone at this table and never said?
cards.followUp: One rule: the envied person has to say what they envied back. There is always something back.

cards.question: Which of your old dreams are you glad didn't come true?
cards.followUp: Ask: did you outgrow it, or did you get talked out of it? Those are different stories — which one is yours?

cards.question: What do we let each other get away with that strangers never would?
cards.followUp: Then ask: is that what friendship is — or is it the one thing friends shouldn't do for each other?

cards.question: If this group of friends has a job in your life, what is it — and who's been doing it?
cards.followUp: Ask who in this group tells you the truth. Then ask when you last actually let them.

cards.question: What's a fight this friendship survived that would have ended a lesser one?
cards.followUp: Ask what actually fixed it — time, an apology, or someone simply refusing to leave. Would the same thing work today?

cards.question: What should we have said to each other years ago that there's still time to say?
cards.followUp: Don't rush past the silence. Someone at the table has an answer — pour them a drink and wait.

packs.title: After Midnight
packs.blurb: For the hour when the plates are cleared, somebody's shoes are off, and nobody is checking the time. The big questions, asked plainly — no reading required, no philosophy degree, just the nerve to answer honestly.

cards.question: Would you want to know the day you're going to die?
cards.followUp: Whoever says no — ask what they're doing differently by not knowing. That's usually the real answer.

cards.question: If you could take a pill that made you permanently content, would you take it?
cards.followUp: Ask the holdouts what they're saving the discontent for. Make them name it out loud.

cards.question: Does your life need to matter to anyone beyond the people who love you for it to be a good life?
cards.followUp: Push once: if a life fully matters to five people, what exactly would 'mattering to history' add?

cards.question: Is there anything you'd refuse to do — even to save someone you love?
cards.followUp: If someone says no, nothing's off-limits: ask them, gently, what that makes the rest of us at this table.

cards.question: You get one completely honest answer to any question, from anyone alive or dead. What do you ask?
cards.followUp: Then the follow-up that matters: why don't you already know? Who could you still ask?

cards.question: If nobody would ever find out, what would you do differently — and what does your answer say about you?
cards.followUp: Flip it: what do you already do right that nobody ever sees? Ask which list came to mind faster.

cards.question: Do you think you're a good person — and what's your actual evidence?
cards.followUp: When people hedge: 'I try' is not evidence. Ask for one witness they'd call, and what that witness would say.

cards.question: What's the closest you've ever come to changing your mind about something huge?
cards.followUp: Ask what finally did it — an argument, a person, or something that happened. Notice how rarely anyone says an argument.

cards.question: If you had to lean one way, would you rather your life be happy or interesting?
cards.followUp: Then ask which one they're actually building. The gap between the answer and the calendar is the conversation.

cards.question: What are you doing with your one short life — and is tonight part of the answer, or a break from it?
cards.followUp: Let everyone laugh first. Then ask it again, quietly. Second answers are better after midnight.
